coredata.dc:description: Considering the important role of tobacco in promoting the economic development of agricultural crops, the high-value recycling of its waste has been a hot topic of interest. In this study, we developed a waste tobacco leaf-driven biomass-based carbon dot (Cur@TB CD) nanozyme with high reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging and anti-inflammatory activities for the amelioration of acute lung injury (ALI) both in vitro and in vivo. The results showed that the Cur@TB CD manifested good superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activity and hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenging ability, making them impressive scavengers of ROS. Moreover, the intracellular analysis of the Cur@TB CD revealed that Cur@TB CD could spontaneously enter the cells via endocytosis and was distributed mainly in the organelles of lysosomes and mitochondria. As a result, intracellular endogenous ROS can be scavenged to inhibit the expression of proinflammatory cytokines (e.g., TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β), alleviating inflammation in lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced ALI in vivo. Importantly, as a biomass-derived product, Cur@TB CD exhibited negligible side effects and acceptable biosafety in vivo, suggesting promising application potential for curing and preventing ALI in clinical applications, with easy-to-prepare, economical, green and environmentally friendly methods.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis procedure of the waste tobacco leave-derived biomass-based antioxidant Cur@TB CD nanozyme via a one-pot hydrothermal method. (B) Schematic diagram of the process by which Cur@TB CD scavenge excess ROS and decrease the expression of inflammatory factors, attenuating inflammation to cure ALI in LPS-induced ALI mice.

Characterization of Cur@TB CD. (A) TEM image of Cur@TB CD. (B) Hydrodynamic size of Cur@TB CD in D.I. water. (C) XRD pattern of the Cur@TB CD. (D) Raman, (E) FT-IR, and (F) absorption spectra of the Cur@TB CD. (G) Normalized fluorescence spectra of Cur@TB CD under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 370 to 420 nm. Inset: Photos of Cur@TB CD before (left) and (right) and after receiving 420 nm UV lamp excitation in D.I. water. (H) EPR spectra of O2 −• (generated from xanthine and xanthine oxidase) + DMPO following incubation with different concentrations of Cur@TB CD at 0.0 (control), 20.0, 50.0, 100.0, and 200.0 μg/mL. (I) EPR spectra of •OH (generated from Fe2+ and H2O2) + DMPO following incubation with different concentrations of Cur@TB CD at 0.0 (control), 20.0, 50.0, 100.0, and 200.0 μg/mL.

Intracellular analysis of Cur@TB CD. (A) Confocal images of the colocalization of Cur@TB CD and LysoTracker@Green DND-26 (lysosome). (B) Confocal images of the colocalization of Cur@TB CD and MitoTracker@Green FM (mitochondria). (C) Flow cytometry analysis for comparing the normalized mean fluorescence intensities of 250.0 ng/mL LPS-untreated (-) and treated (+) RAW 264.7 macrophages following incubation with Cur@TB CD at concentrations ranging from 0.0 to 100.0 μg/mL. (D) Fluorescence images of RAW 264.7 macrophages incubated with 20.0 μM DCFH-DA and (a) PBS, (b) LPS, (c) LPS + 10.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD, (d) LPS + 20.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD, (e) LPS + 40.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD, or (f) LPS + 60.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD. The concentration of LPS was 250.0 ng/mL. (E–G) The expression concentrations of TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-1β in 50.0 ng/mL LPS-treated RAW 264.7 macrophages following incubation with different concentrations of Cur@TB CD ranging from 0.0 to 100.0 μg/mL. Statistical comparisons between two groups were performed via one-way ANOVA. The data were expressed as the means ± SDs (n = 3).

Evaluation of the anti-inflammatory effect of Cur@TB CD on LPS-induced ALI in mice. (A) H&E staining images of lung tissues from healthy mice (control group), LPS-induced ALI mice (LPS group), Cur@TB CD-treated mice (LPS + Cur@TB CD group) and DXMS (LPS + DXMS group)-treated mice. (B) DAPI- and DHE-stained images of the lung tissues subjected to the indicated treatments described above. (C & D) Concentrations of TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-1β in lung tissues and BALF from the indicated treatment groups. Statistical comparisons between two groups were performed via one-way ANOVA. The data were expressed as the means ± SDs (n = 3).

In vivo biosafety analysis of Cur@TB CD. (A) H&E staining images of the liver, spleen, kidneys, heart, and lungs of healthy mice after single intratracheal instillation of 20.0 mg/kg Cur@TB CD for 0.0 (control), 7.0, and 30.0 days, respectively. (B) Hemolysis rates of red blood cells following incubation with 1: saline (positive), 2: D.I. water (negative), 3--8: 50.0, 100.0, 150.0, 200.0, 250.0, and 300.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD. Inset: corresponding photos of the above treated red blood cells. (C-G) Serum levels of indicators (ALT, AST, ALP, BUN and CR) of liver and kidney functions in the above healthy mice after a single intratracheal instillation of 20.0 mg/kg Cur@TB CD at 0.0 (control), 7.0, and 30.0 days, respectively. Statistical comparisons between two groups were performed via one-way ANOVA. The data were expressed as the means ± SDs (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Waste tobacco leaf-driven carbon dot nanozymes with high reactive oxygen species scavenging and anti-inflammatory activities for the amelioration of acute lung injury

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Considering the important role of tobacco in promoting the economic development of agricultural crops, the high-value recycling of its waste has been a hot topic of interest. In this study, we developed a waste tobacco leaf-driven biomass-based carbon dot (Cur@TB CD) nanozyme with high reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging and anti-inflammatory activities for the amelioration of acute lung injury (ALI) both in vitro and in vivo. The results showed that the Cur@TB CD manifested good superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activity and hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenging ability, making them impressive scavengers of ROS. Moreover, the intracellular analysis of the Cur@TB CD revealed that Cur@TB CD could spontaneously enter the cells via endocytosis and was distributed mainly in the organelles of lysosomes and mitochondria. As a result, intracellular endogenous ROS can be scavenged to inhibit the expression of proinflammatory cytokines (e.g., TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β), alleviating inflammation in lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced ALI in vivo. Importantly, as a biomass-derived product, Cur@TB CD exhibited negligible side effects and acceptable biosafety in vivo, suggesting promising application potential for curing and preventing ALI in clinical applications, with easy-to-prepare, economical, green and environmentally friendly methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Tobacco, as a cash crop, is widely planted in the northern and southern regions of China, and its planting area ranks first in the world (Ma et al., 2023; Zou et al., 2021). Notably, many waste tobacco byproducts (e.g., leaves and stems) can be produced during the process of tobacco cultivation and processing, accounting for approximately 25 % of the total tobacco production, leading to serious resource waste and environmental pollution (Guo et al., 2024; Hu et al., 2023; Tan et al., 2023). It is conceivable that the added value of waste tobacco byproducts will significantly increase if they can be effectively utilized. Therefore, the high-value utilization of waste plant byproducts into versatile biomass-based carbon dot (CD) is a promising way to solve the waste of resources because biomass-based CD usually have broader applications in bio/chemical sensing, photocatalysis, disease diagnosis and treatment, etc. (Guo et al., 2022; Liang et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022). Notably, biomass-based CD are usually more popular in biomedical applications because of their relatively greater biosafety and lower side effects than CD synthesized from regular chemical reagents (Liu et al., 2024). Inspired by these findings, a new avenue for the high-value use of tobacco waste may be the exploitation of waste tobacco as a resource to develop versatile biomass-based CD for biomedical applications, which is highly important for achieving a global carbon-neutral strategy.

For a long time, smoking has been considered a serious threat that can cause serious respiratory diseases, such as acute lung injury (ALI), which is thought to be directly related to the overproduction of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the process of burning tobacco, leading to oxidative damage in the lungs and even the whole body (Ji et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2024). The current treatment for ALI is based mainly on the use of glucocorticoids or nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate oxidase inhibitors, which can downregulate inflammatory factors or inhale vasodilator gases to increase blood flow to the ventilation zone, leading to the drawbacks of high cost, poor prognosis, failure to fundamentally neutralize the excess ROS surrounding the inflammatory zone, etc. (Harijith et al., 2017; Yuan et al., 2022). Recently, with the rapid development of nanotechnology, various antioxidant nanozymes have been developed to scavenge ROS to perform anti-inflammatory activities to ameliorate ALI in a highly efficient and low-cost way (Liu et al., 2021; Yin et al., 2024). Among them, several recent studies have explored antioxidant CD nanozymes to scavenge intracellular ROS for the treatment of inflammation-related diseases because of their relatively excellent biocompatibility and biosafety in vivo, indicating promising prospects for biomedical applications (Dong et al., 2021; Dong et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2023). In particular, Zhao et al. exploited Armeniacae Semen Amarum Carbonisata as a biomass resource to synthesize biomass-based CD with remarkable anti-inflammatory effects against ALI in vivo (Zhao et al., 2022). These findings provide inspiration for the development of waste tobacco leaf-driven biomass-based antioxidant CD nanozymes with high ROS scavenging and anti-inflammatory activities for ameliorating ALI in vivo as well as meeting the requirements of green chemistry, but no concerns remain.

According to the above analysis, we herein focused on designing a biomass-based antioxidant CD nanozyme with ROS scavenging and anti-inflammatory activities driven by waste tobacco byproducts to ameliorate ALI in vivo. To achieve this goal, we synthesized a biomass-based antioxidant CD nanozyme (Cur@TB CD) via the classical one-pot solvothermal method, which is based on waste tobacco leaves supplemented with curcumin (Cur) and glutathione (GSH) in formamide ( Fig. 1). Through in-depth analysis, the Cur@TB CD exhibited good superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activity, hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenging ability and dual-wavelength fluorescence emission characteristics. Moreover, Cur@TB CD could enter cells via endocytosis to be distributed mainly in the organelles of lysosomes and mitochondria, remarkably decreasing the level of intracellular endogenous ROS to inhibit the expression of proinflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-1β to alleviate inflammation. Interestingly, this anti-inflammatory effect of Cur@TB CD was also demonstrated in LPS-induced ALI mice through intratracheal instillation, which has ignorable toxicity and acceptable biosafety in vivo, indicating that Cur@TB CD have great potential for curing ALI in vivo and may help smokers prevent ALI through, for example, developing functionalized cigarette filters.

All the chemical and biological reagents used in this work were purchased directly from the suppliers without any treatment. Cur, GSH, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), xanthine oxidase, lipopolysaccharide (LPS) and 2’7’-dichlorofluorescence (DCFH-DA) were purchased from SigmaAldrich Chemical Corporation (St. Louis, MO, USA). Formamide, xanthine, and FeSO4 ·7 H2O (Fe2 +) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). The SOD assay kit-WST (WST-1) was purchased from Dojindo (Japan) for measuring the SOD enzyme activity of Cur@TB CD via the inhibition rate method. The SOD assay kit WST-8 (WST-8) was purchased from Grace Biologicals (Suzhou, China) for quantifying the SOD enzyme activity of the Cur@TB CD. A Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was purchased from Vazyme Biotech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). LysoTracker@Green DND-26 and MitoTracker@Green FM were purchased from Invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA, USA). RAW 264.7 mouse monocytic macrophage leukemia cell lines (RAW 264.7 macrophages), human hepatocellular carcinoma HepG2 cell lines and human lung adenocarcinoma A549 cell lines were obtained from the Cell Bank of the Shanghai Institutes for Biological Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). The TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-1β ELISA kits were purchased from Ruixin Biotechnology (Fujian, China). A hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining kit and 4’,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) and dihydroethidium (DHE) staining solutions were obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology. The male C57BL/6 mice used in this work were acquired from the Laboratory Animal Centre of Anhui Medical University (Anhui, China), and all the animal experiments were reviewed and approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of Anhui Medical University (No. LLSC20230914). Throughout the experiment, without special instructions, all solutions were prepared with deionized (D.I.) water.

A field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI Tal os F200X, USA) was used to acquire transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the Cur@TB CD. A Zetasizer (Nano-Z, Malvern, UK) was used to profile the zeta potential and hydrodynamic size of the Cur@TB CD. A carry 50 UV spectrometer (Varian, USA) was used to record all the absorption spectra. An F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) was used to obtain the fluorescence spectra. An infrared spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) was used to detect the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of the Cur@TB CD. An ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Electron, USA) was used for elemental analysis of the Cur@TB CD. A Bruker D8 Advance instrument (Germany) was used to acquire X-ray diffraction (XRD) data for the Cur@TB CD. A Renishaw InVia instrument (Renishaw, UK) was used to collect the Raman data of the Cur@TB CD. A Bruker A300–10/12 (Bruker, Saarbrücken, Germany) was used to measure the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of the ROS. A microplate reader (680, Bio-Rad, USA) was used to conduct the CCK-8 assay. A Coulter FC-500 flow cytometer (Beckman Coulter, USA) was used to carry out the flow cytometry analysis. The cell images were collected via fluorescence microscopy (Nikon, Japan) and a TCS SP5 laser scanning confocal microscope (Leica, Germany).

Cur@TB CD were synthesized via a one-pot solvothermal method. Specifically, 0.1 g of spent waste tobacco leaf powder, 0.3 g of GSH and 0.005 g of Cur were added to 10.0 mL of formamide. To fully dissolve the mixture, it was first vortexed and then sonicated for 30.0 min. At this point, the resulting mixture was transferred to a tetrafluoroethylene reactor and heated at 180.0 °C for 8.0 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the solution was extracted and filtered through a 0.22 μm organic filter membrane to remove large particles and precipitates. The resulting dark brown filtrate was further dialyzed through a cellulose dialysis bag (MW = 500.0) in D.I. water for 7.0 days with water changes every 24.0 h. Finally, the dialysate was filtered through a 0.22 μm aqueous membrane and freeze-dried to obtain a powdered sample, which was stored in a decorator for subsequent experiments.

To evaluate the ability of Cur@TB CD to scavenge O2 −•, O2 −• was first generated from 2.5 mM xanthine and 0.25 U/mL xanthine oxidase at room temperature (Yang et al., 2009), which were further incubated with DMPO together with Cur@TB CD at 0.0 (control), 20.0, 50.0, 100.0, and 200.0 μg/mL. To evaluate the scavenging ability of Cur@TB CD for •OH, •OH was first generated from Fe2+ and H2O2 at room temperature (Zhang et al., 2016), which were further incubated with DMPO together with Cur@TB CD at 0.0 (control), 20.0, 50.0, 100.0, and 200.0 μg/mL. All the samples were subsequently subjected to EPR measurements under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging analysis of Cur@TB CD
ce:section.ce:para: RAW 264.7 macrophages were seeded into a 35.0 mm confocal dish (glass bottom dish) for culture overnight in a 37.0°C humidified incubator (5 % CO2 and 95 % air). To optimize the incubation concentration, these RAW 264.7 macrophages were incubated with Cur@TB CD at 0.0 (control), 25.0, 50.0, 100.0, and 150.0 μg/mL in 2.0 mL Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, V/V) at 37.0°C for 24.0 h. To optimize the incubation time, these RAW 264.7 macrophages were incubated with 100.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD in 2.0 mL DMEM supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, V/V) at 37.0°C for 0.0 (control), 4.0, 8.0, 12.0, 24.0, and 36.0 h, respectively. For the colocalization assay, these RAW 264.7 macrophages were first incubated with 2.0 mL of DMEM supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, V/V) containing 100.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD for 24.0 h and then washed with sterile PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) three times. In turn, these treated RAW 264.7 macrophages were further incubated with a lysosomal tracker (LysoTracker@Green DND-26, 50.0 nM) and a mitochondrial tracker (Mito-Tracker@Green FM, 100.0 nM) at 37.0°C for another 30.0 min, respectively. After that, all the RAW 264.7 macrophages were washed with sterile PBS (pH = 7.4) three times for cell imaging analysis via a TCS SP5 laser scanning confocal microscope (Leica, Germany).

RAW 264.7 macrophages were seeded into a 96-well plate and cultured overnight in a 37.0°C humidified incubator (5 % CO2 and 95 % air). After that, these RAW 264.7 macrophages were incubated with DMEM supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, V/V) containing 20.0 μM DCFH-DA for 30.0 min and then washed with sterile PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) three times. Next, these RAW 264.7 macrophages were untreated (control) and incubated with 250.0 ng/mL LPS for 90.0 min, 10.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD for 3.0 h + 250.0 ng/mL LPS for 90.0 min, 20.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD for 3.0 h + 250.0 ng/mL LPS for 90.0 min, 40.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD for 3.0 h + 250.0 ng/mL LPS for 90.0 min, and 60.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD for 3.0 h + 250.0 ng/mL LPS for 90.0 min. Finally, all RAW 264.7 macrophages were washed with sterile PBS (pH = 7.4) three times to carry out the cell imaging analysis on a fluorescence microscope (Nikon, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo modeling of acute lung injury
ce:section.ce:para: Sixteen healthy male C57BL/6 mice, aged 8.0–10.0 weeks and weighing approximately 20.0–25.0 g, were randomly assigned to four experimental groups (n = 4 per group): control (healthy mice), ALI model, LPS + Cur@TB CD treatment, and LPS + dexamethasone (DXMS) treatment. ALI was induced in the experimental groups (excluding the healthy controls) via intratracheal instillation of LPS (5.0 mg/kg in sterile saline) under mild anesthesia according to a previously reported method (Yuan et al., 2022). At 5.0 min post-LPS administration, the mice in the LPS + Cur@TB CD group received an intratracheal instillation of Cur@TB CD (20.0 mg/kg), whereas the mice in the LPS + DXMS group were intratracheally administered DXMS (5.0 mg/kg). After 24.0 h of treatment, all the mice were euthanized with an overdose of isoflurane, and their lung tissues were harvested for further analysis. Moreover, the levels of proinflammatory cytokines, including TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-1β, in the lung tissue were quantified via ELISA. Additional lung tissue sections were subjected to histological analysis via H&E staining, DAPI staining (excitation: 358 nm, emission: 450–470 nm) and DHE staining (excitation: 518 nm, emission: 590–620 nm) to assess cellularity, inflammation and ROS generation, respectively.

For long-term safety evaluation, we conducted a 30.0 days biosafety study in which Cur@TB CD (20.0 mg/kg) were administered to mice, and histopathological analysis and biochemical assays were performed. No significant adverse effects on major organs (liver, kidneys, heart, lungs, or spleen) or liver or kidney function (alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), blood urea nitrogen (BUN) or creatinine (CR)) were observed, confirming the long-term safety of the Cur@TB CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Cur@TB CD
ce:section.ce:para: Waste tobacco leaf-based biomass nanozyme Cur@TB CD with the assistance of Cur, GSH and formamide were synthesized via the classical one-pot hydrothermal method. The TEM image clearly shows that the Cur@TB CD were well dispersed ( Fig. 2A), with an average hydrodynamic size of ∼ 5.65 nm (Fig. 2B). The distinct peak at approximately 2θ = ∼ 20.5° in the XRD pattern of the Cur@TB CD belongs to the (002) facet of graphite carbon (Fig. 2C) (Liu et al., 2023). Moreover, the weaker intensity of the G band at ∼ 1549 cm−1 than the D band at ∼ 1333 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum of the Cur@TB CD indicated many structural defects inside the Cur@TB CD (Fig. 2D) (Zhang et al., 2021). These results showed that Cur@TB CD were successfully synthesized. XPS analysis revealed that the Cur@TB CD contained mainly C, O, N, and S (Fig. S1). A deep profile revealed two peaks at 286.04 eV and 284.50 eV in the high-resolution binding energy spectrum of C 1 s, which were assigned to O-CO and CC/C-C, respectively, suggesting a representative sp2 orbital hybridization of the carbon atoms inside the Cur@TB CD (Chen et al., 2024). Moreover, there was a typical peak at 532.28 eV belonging to C-O in the high-resolution binding energy O 1 s spectrum (Zhao et al., 2021). These findings also revealed that the relatively high content of C (59.44 %) and O (37.1 %) inside the Cur@TB CD was because the main components of tobacco are carbohydrates. In addition, there were two peaks at 400.10 eV and 398.27 eV corresponding to C-N = C and N = N, respectively (Yan et al., 2019), whereas the other two peaks at 167.65 eV and 163.99 eV were attributed to S 2p3/2 and oxidized sulfur, respectively (Yan et al., 2023). These findings from the above XPS analysis were further verified by FT-IR measurements (Fig. 2E). For example, the broad peak at 3200–3400 cm−1 was attributed to N–H and O–H stretching vibrations (Louleb et al., 2020). The peak at 1648 cm−1 was attributed to CN/CC stretching vibrations, the peak at 1154 cm−1 resulted from C-O stretching vibrations (Wang et al., 2018), the peak at 1387 cm−1 originated from O-H in-plane deformation vibration or C-N stretching vibration, and the peak at 1040 cm−1 was attributed to oxidized sulfur bonds (Duan et al., 2023). According to the results above, the Cur@TB CD likely possessed abundant functional groups, such as carbonyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups, on their surface, which increased the water solubility of the Cur@TB CD.

Next, the absorption and fluorescence properties of the Cur@TB CD were investigated in D.I. water. As depicted in Fig. 2F, there were two obvious absorption bands at 400–450 nm and 625–675 nm, which resulted from the π–π* and n–π* leaps of aromatic CO and CN bonds, respectively (Ge et al., 2021). Interestingly, Cur@TB CD exhibited typical dual-wavelength fluorescence emission under a single-excitation wavelength (Fig. 2G), with an excitation-independent feature, implying that the homogeneous surface states of the sp2 clusters caused highly uniform sizes of Cur@TB CD (Roy et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2020). Notably, Cur@TB CD showed good stability because of the insignificant fluorescence changes before and after incubation with D.I. water (Fig. S2), PBS (Fig. S3), and DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS (V/V, Fig. S4) for 20.0 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Profiling the ablation of Cur@TB CD for ROS
ce:section.ce:para: To profile the ablation of Cur@TB CD for ROS, the classical radical scavenger named 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was first applied to explore the scavenging ability of Cur@TB CD toward O2 −• by measuring their electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) signals. As shown in Fig. 2H, the initial four typical EPR peaks of O2 −• with an intensity ratio of approximately 1:1:1:1 were gradually eliminated by increasing the concentration of Cur@TB CD until they completely disappeared at 200.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD, indicating prominent O2 −• ablation of the Cur@TB CD (Yu et al., 2020). This finding implied that the Cur@TB CD exhibited SOD-like activity, which was confirmed by the commercial assay kit WST-1 (Ma et al., 2024). Moreover, the other commercial assay kit, WST-8, was employed to evaluate the SOD activity of the Cur@TB CD, which was 614.70 U/mg (Fig. S5 & Note S1). The biomass-based Cur@TB CD synthesized from discarded tobacco leaf are more distinctive and attractive due to their excellent biocompatibility, low side-effects and low price as compared to other enzymes reported to scavenge ROS. Meanwhile, as compared with other reported nanozymes, the SOD-like activity of Cur@TB CD was comparable or even greater (Table S1). Considering that •OH also plays a significant role in inflammation, the ablation of Cur@TB CD toward •OH was investigated by measuring four typical DMPO-driven EPR peaks of •OH at an intensity ratio of approximately 1:2:2:1 (Shen et al., 2022). Like O2 −•, Cur@TB CD exhibited concentration-dependent EPR signal ablation of •OH, and •OH was completely scavenged when the concentration of Cur@TB CD reached 200.0 μg/mL (Fig. 2I), indicating that Cur@TB CD also had a prominent elimination ability toward •OH. Based on these findings, it was surprising that this proposed waste tobacco leaf-based biomass Cur@TB CD resulted in high ablation of ROS, suggesting that Cur@TB CD are promising antioxidants for biological applications.

Encouraged by the results above, the ability of the Cur@TB CD to scavenge intracellular ROS was subsequently examined. Before this, the cytotoxicity of the Cur@TB CD was first tested on RAW 264.7 macrophages and HepG2 and A549 cells via a standard CCK-8 cell counting kit assay, which revealed the excellent biocompatibility of the Cur@TB CD because there was a prominent cell viability of over 100 % after incubation for over 24.0 h (Fig. S6 & Note S2). As evidence of concept, the ability of Cur@TB CD to enter living cells was first tested in RAW 264.7 macrophages. After the incubation of RAW 264.7 macrophages with varying concentrations of Cur@TB CD (0.0, 25.0, 50.0, 100.0, and 150.0 μg/mL) for different durations (0.0, 4.0, 8.0, 12.0, 24.0, and 36.0 h), the maximal intracellular red fluorescence signals from the Cur@TB CD appeared at 100.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD after 24.0 h of incubation (Figs. S7 & S8). Therefore, the intracellular ROS scavenging analysis could be performed after exploiting 100.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD for 24.0 h of incubation. By performing colocalization assays of Cur@TB CD with lysosomal and mitochondrial trackers, Cur@TB CD were shown to accumulate in lysosomes, with a Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) of 0.9245 ( Fig. 3A), whereas in mitochondria, the PCC was 0.7593 (Fig. 3B). These findings might be due to the primary phagocytosis of lysosomes for foreign substances, resulting in the main distribution of Cur@TB CD in lysosomes and the subsequent continuous dispersion of Cur@TB CD to enter mitochondria (Liu et al., 2020), suggesting that Cur@TB CD could facilitate the scavenging of ROS, particularly O2 −• produced in mitochondria. Moreover, the Cur@TB CD were more interested in entering LPS-treated RAW 264.7 macrophages because the fluorescence signal of the Cur@TB CD in the LPS-treated cells was much stronger than that in the untreated cells (Fig. 3C). These results revealed that Cur@TB CD have the potential to target inflammation, as LPS was a typical initiator for the expression of chemokines and inflammatory factors in cells to induce a strong inflammatory response (John and Raza, 2021).

The effect of the Cur@TB CD on the scavenging of intracellular ROS was next explored in RAW 264.7 macrophages, and the intracellular ROS levels were determined with DCFH-DA (a ROS-specific fluorescence indicator) as the fluorescence indicator (Wang et al., 2016). As shown in Fig. 3D, there were obvious ROS fluorescence signals in the LPS-treated RAW 264.7 macrophages compared with those in the control, which could be significantly inhibited in the presence of the Cur@TB CD in a Cur@TB CD concentration-dependent manner, clearly indicating the strong intracellular ROS scavenging ability of the Cur@TB CD. Proinflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-1β, are generally overexpressed during inflammation through unregulated immune responses (Li et al., 2023). Given this point, the ability of the Cur@TB CD to promote the anti-inflammatory effect of intracellular ROS could be assessed by measuring the expression levels of TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-1β in LPS-treated RAW 264.7 macrophages as an inflammation model (El-Seedi et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 3E, treatment with Cur@TB CD dose-dependently reduced the expression levels of all three proinflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β) in LPS-stimulated RAW 264.7 macrophages. These findings collectively suggest that Cur@TB CDs exert potent anti-inflammatory effects through the suppression of proinflammatory cytokine production. Considering that the intracellular stability of Cur@TB CD directly affects their ability to regulate the levels of endogenous ROS and proinflammatory cytokines (Han et al., 2018), the residence time of the intracellular Cur@TB CD was explored via flow cytometry. The corresponding results suggested that the intracellular fluorescence signals of the Cur@TB CD remained greater than 88.7 % even after 24.0 h (Fig. S9 & Note S3), indicating that the Cur@TB CD could remain in the cells for up to 24.0 h after entering the cells without being excreted. These findings suggested that the Cur@TB CD were relatively stable in the intracellular environment, which could be beneficial for the subsequent long-term elimination of intracellular ROS and attenuation of inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-inflammatory effects of Cur@TB CD in LPS-induced ALI mice
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro studies have revealed that Cur@TB CD exhibited strong ROS scavenging capabilities, as evidenced by significantly reducing ROS levels in treated cells and inhibiting the overexpression of inflammatory cytokines. Encouraged from these results, we subsequently investigated the effects of Cur@TB CD in an in vivo LPS-induced ALI mouse model. Currently, dexamethasone (DXMS) is often used to treat ALI in the clinic, so we selected DXMS as a positive control to evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of Cur@TB CD for ALI (García-Fernández et al., 2021). First, we induced ALI via intratracheal instillation of LPS into mice and then constructed a mouse ALI model. After 5.0 min of LPS treatment, the mice were intratracheally instilled with the clinical drugs DXMS (5.0 mg/kg) and Cur@TB CD (20.0 mg/kg) for the treatment of ALI, and the dosage of intratracheal instillation was based on the body weights of the mice. After 24.0 h of treatment, all the mice were euthanized with an overdose of isoflurane, and their lung tissues were collected. H&E staining of the lung tissues of healthy mice revealed that, compared with healthy control mice, LPS-treated mice presented significant pathological changes, including diffuse cellular damage, marked thickening of alveolar walls, and significant infiltration of neutrophils. These pathological features indicated that we successfully constructed an ALI model ( Fig. 4A). Excessive ROS during the development of ALI are the main cause of lung failure; therefore, the clearance of ROS is the key to the treatment of ALI. To observe the ability of Cur@TB CD to scavenge ROS in vivo, the O2 −• probe dihydroethidium (DHE) was used to detect O2 −• levels in lung tissues. The intensity of red fluorescence of DHE staining indirectly reflects the degree of O2 −• content in vivo, and an elevated O2 −• content results in a stronger intensity of red fluorescence (Lee et al., 2020). By observing the results of DHE staining, we found that the lung tissues emitted obvious red fluorescence after LPS treatment, indicating that the O2 −• content in the lung tissues was high. However, after Cur@TB CD and DXMS treatment, the red fluorescence decreased to very weak values, which indicated that the Cur@TB CD significantly inhibited the production of excess ROS in the lung tissue (Fig. 4B).

In addition to excess ROS, three proinflammatory factors, TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-1β, are associated with the development of inflammation and tissue damage, and a reduction in these inflammatory factors is associated with the successful treatment of ALI (Ji et al., 2016). The levels of TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-1β were significantly greater in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF) and lung tissue after LPS treatment. However, the levels of inflammatory factors were significantly lower after treatment with Cur@TB CD and DXMS (Fig. 4C & 4D). In addition, the dry-to-wet (W/D) ratio of the lungs after lung injury is an important index for evaluating the water content of the lungs; the W/D ratios of the lungs of the LPS-treated mice were much greater than the normal values, and pulmonary edema was significantly improved after treatment with both Cur@TB CD and the clinical drug DXMS (Fig. S10 & Note S4). The degree of lung histopathology could be assessed by the lung histopathology score, which revealed significant lung injury after treatment with LPS, with a lung histopathology score ranging from 8.0–9.0 (Fig. S11 & Note S5, (Chang et al., 2018). However, the ALI recovered and improved after treatment with Cur@TB CD and DXMS, with scores ranging from 1.0–3.0. These results suggest that Cur@TB CD successfully treat the ALI model, achieving therapeutic effects comparable to those of the clinical drug DXMS and showing great potential in inflammatory therapy. In summary, the anti-inflammatory effects of the in vitro experiments confirmed that the Cur@TB CD scavenged ROS and inhibited the overexpression of inflammatory factors at the cellular levels, whereas the in vivo experiments verified that the Cur@TB CD had the same efficacy in complex physiological environments. The in vivo and in vitro experiments confirmed the anti-inflammatory effects of the Cur@TB CD, laying a scientific foundation for the further development and application of the Cur@TB CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biosafety analysis of Cur@TB CD
ce:section.ce:para: The safety of therapeutic nanomedicines in vivo is crucial, and for this reason, we evaluated the biosafety of Cur@TB CD in an LPS-induced ALI mouse model. Nine healthy male C57BL/6 mice, aged 8.0–10.0 weeks and weighing approximately 20.0–25.0 g, were used in this study. The mice were housed under standard laboratory conditions, including a 12.0 h light/dark cycle, controlled temperature and humidity, and ad libitum access to food and water. After one week of acclimatization, the mice were randomly assigned to three groups (n = 3 per group). The first group received a single intratracheal installation of Cur@TB CD at a dose of 20.0 mg/kg in PBS and was monitored for 7.0 days. The second group received a single intratracheal instillation of Cur@TB CD at a dose of 20.0 mg/kg in PBS and was monitored for 30.0 days. The third group received a single intratracheal instillation of an equal volume of PBS and was monitored for 30.0 days. Then, histological examination with classical H&E staining was performed on major organs, such as the liver, spleen, kidneys, heart and lungs. As shown in Fig. 5A, even after 30.0 days of single intratracheal instillation of Cur@TB CD, there were no significant adverse effects on these major organs. Moreover, after incubating different concentrations of Cur@TB CD with red blood cells for 3.0 h, there were insignificant effects on the state of the red blood cells as well as hemolysis, even at 300.0 μg/mL Cur@TB CD (Fig. 5B, (Wang et al., 2024). The results of the hemolysis test indicated that the Cur@TB CD have good hemocompatibility. Apart from these, the in vivo effects of Cur@TB CD on liver function indicators (i.e., alanine aminotransferase (ALT, Fig. 5C), aspartate aminotransferase (AST, Fig. 5D), and alkaline phosphatase (ALP, Fig. 5E)) and renal function indicators (i.e., blood urea nitrogen (BUN) & creatinine (CR)) were evaluated as well (Fig. 5F & 5 G). Similar to H&E staining, all these tested indicators exhibited negligible changes before and after receiving a single intratracheal instillation of Cur@TB CD for 30.0 days, suggesting that Cur@TB CD did not damage liver or kidney function in vivo (Zhang et al., 2023). Based on the above analysis, the proposed waste tobacco leaf-based biomass Cur@TB CD were biocompatible and negligible in treating ALI in vivo, at least on the basis of short-term observations over a one-month period.

In summary, this study demonstrated the great potential of exploring waste tobacco leaves as a biomass resource to develop a novel biomass-derived antioxidant Cur@TB CD nanozyme for realizing efficient, biosafe, economical, green and environmentally friendly treatment of LPS-induced ALI-infected mice for the first time. Through the classical one-pot solvothermal method, these biomass-based Cur@TB CD were successfully synthesized by exploiting waste tobacco leaves assisted by Cur and GSH in formamide as precursors. Cur@TB CD can spontaneously enter the lysosomes and mitochondria of infected cells, initiating SOD-like activity and •OH scavenging to scavenge abnormal ROS and inhibit the overexpression of inflammatory factors (e.g., TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-1β), thereby achieving significant anti-inflammatory effects both in vitro and in vivo, for example, by curing LPS-induced ALI in mice. In addition to the acute therapeutic effects observed in the 24.0 h in vivo studies, we also conducted a comprehensive 30.0 days biosafety study to evaluate the long-term safety of Cur@TB CD. The results of this study indicated that Cur@TB CD are safe for prolonged use, showing no toxicity to major organs such as the liver, kidneys, heart, or lungs. Furthermore, there were no significant changes in liver or kidney function markers (ALT, AST, BUN, and CR), demonstrating that Cur@TB CD do not cause organ damage or systemic toxicity after long-term administration. This study presents a scalable strategy to waste tobacco byproducts through the development of biosafe and economic biomass-derived materials for ameliorating ALI-related diseases, which may also help to decrease the number of ALI among smokers through the development of functionalized cigarette filters.
